facts.title: Quadriceps
facts.body: The quadriceps straighten the knee and help control knee position during lower-body lifts.

facts.title: Hamstrings
facts.body: The hamstrings cross the hip and knee, so hip hinges and curls train different jobs of the same region.

facts.title: Glutes
facts.body: The glutes drive hip extension and help position the pelvis during lower-body exercises.

facts.title: Adductors
facts.body: The adductors assist hip motion and can contribute strongly during squats and lunges.

facts.title: Spine and Core
facts.body: The spine works best when the torso can brace while the hips and shoulders move around it.

facts.title: Back and Lats
facts.body: Pulling movements coordinate the shoulder joint, scapula, upper back, and arm muscles.

facts.title: Chest
facts.body: Pressing movements use the chest with the shoulder and triceps rather than as an isolated body part.

facts.title: Shoulders
facts.body: The shoulder is highly mobile, so good pressing and pulling depends on scapular control.

facts.title: Biceps
facts.body: The biceps flex the elbow and also demonstrate how muscles shorten, lengthen, and transmit force.

facts.title: Triceps
facts.body: The triceps extend the elbow and support pressing movements.

facts.title: Forearms and Wrists
facts.body: Wrist and hand position can change how stress is distributed through the forearm and elbow.

facts.title: Skeletal System and Joints
facts.body: Bones, cartilage, ligaments, and synovial joints create the structure that muscles pull against.

facts.title: Nervous System and Motor Control
facts.body: Motor control depends on the nervous system coordinating timing, attention, and repeated practice.

facts.title: Breathing
facts.body: Breathing during rest can shift the body toward recovery and prepare the next set.

facts.title: Cardiovascular/Lymphatic Recovery
facts.body: Heart rate, blood flow, lymph, and inflammation all affect how you feel between sets.

facts.title: Stress and Recovery
facts.body: Rest periods are a chance to downshift stress and improve readiness for the next effort.

facts.title: Pelvis and Hips
facts.body: The pelvis links the spine and legs, making hip position important in lower-body training.

facts.title: Muscle Contraction
facts.body: Muscle fibers can shorten, lengthen under tension, or hold position isometrically.
